serving.natural_language_portion: A typical shared serving of stir-fried black fungus with celery at a Chinese restaurant is approximately 200 g, representing one shared side dish for two to three diners.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Mushrooms, wood ear, dried (FDC ID 168436) and Celery, raw (FDC ID 169988), combined with standard stir-fry oil addition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for stir-fried black fungus with celery exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Values estimated by combining USDA FDC data for rehydrated wood ear mushroom (Auricularia auricula-judae) and raw celery in approximate proportions (60% fungus, 30% celery, 10% oil/seasoning contribution), adjusted for stir-fry cooking with ~10 g vegetable oil per 100 g cooked dish. Rehydration factor applied to dried mushroom data. Values are best estimates and should be verified against a laboratory analysis or HPB Singapore Foodo if a matching entry becomes available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on non-starchy vegetables and fungi category
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried black fungus with celery. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of non-starchy vegetables and edible fungi as low-GI foods (GI typically 10–20). Wood ear mushroom is predominantly water and dietary fibre with minimal digestible carbohydrate, placing it firmly in the low-GI category. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as (15 × 5.1 × 2.0) / 100 = 1.5, rounded to one decimal place.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g restaurant serving contributes approximately 560 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or low-sodium seasoning and limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low digestible carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and only 5.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g, this dish has a negligible glycemic load and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a low-carbohydrate vegetable side dish; the dietary fibre from wood ear fungus (2.8 g per 100 g) further attenuates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, cholesterol-free, and high dietary fibre from wood ear fungus
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is cholesterol-free and low in saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g); wood ear fungus contains beta-glucan polysaccharides associated with modest LDL-cholesterol reduction, making this a heart-friendly vegetable dish when prepared with minimal oil and sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Black Fungus with Celery (山芹炒木耳, Shān qín chǎo mù ěr) is a classic Chinese vegetable side dish featuring rehydrated black wood ear mushroom (Auricularia auricula-judae) and Chinese celery (Apium graveolens var. secalinum) quickly stir-fried in a wok with garlic, vegetable oil, soy sauce, and salt. The dish has a distinctive crunchy, gelatinous texture from the wood ear fungus and a fresh, slightly bitter flavour from the Chinese celery. It is a staple in Chinese home cooking and is commonly served at Chinese restaurants in Singapore, including establishments such as Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories (approximately 62 kcal per 100 g), very low in carbohydrates (5.1 g per 100 g), and provides meaningful dietary fibre (2.8 g per 100 g) primarily from the beta-glucan polysaccharides in wood ear fungus. It is cholesterol-free and low in saturated fat. The main nutritional concern is sodium from soy sauce seasoning, which can reach 280 mg per 100 g in restaurant preparations. Clinically, the dish is well-suited for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic index (estimated GI ~15) and low glycemic load. The dietary fibre content supports gut health and may contribute to modest LDL-cholesterol reduction. The dish is naturally vegan, halal, and dairy-free, though it contains soy allergens from soy sauce. It is typically served as a shared side dish alongside rice or other mains.

confidence_reason: No exact match for this composite dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA FDC data for rehydrated wood ear mushroom and raw celery with a standard stir-fry oil addition. GI was estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database non-starchy vegetable and fungi category. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component ingredients are well-characterised in approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (wood ear mushroom FDC ID 168436, celery FDC ID 169988) combined with a standard stir-fry oil contribution; no direct composite dish entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FDC. Values should be updated if a direct laboratory analysis or HPB Foodo entry becomes available. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — vegetable oil used in stir-frying contains negligible trans fat per USDA FDC data). 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — dish is entirely plant-based with no animal-derived ingredients). 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — dish contains no animal products; fungi do not provide bioavailable B12). 5. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of non-starchy vegetables and edible fungi; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was located. 6. Sodium value of 280 mg per 100 g reflects a typical restaurant preparation with soy sauce; home-cooked versions may have lower sodium. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FDC component data and are approximate.
